coredata.dc:title: 4 Clinical applications of nanostructured drug delivery systems From basic research to translational medicine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Core-Shell Nanostructures for Drug Delivery and Theranostics

Sousa, João
Pais, Alberto
Vitorino, Carla
coredata.dc:description: Nanotechnology has become a driving force for innovation in medicine and modern healthcare, boosting advances in therapeutics, biosensors, and imaging systems. This is evidenced by the number of studies encompassing clinical applications of nanostructure-based formulations, which has been increasingly expanding. Cancer, central nervous system, and immune disorders are the leading areas in the research and development of new and better medicines, along with an early diagnosis. This chapter presents an overview of the clinical applications of nanosystems, ranging from basic research to translational medicine. In spite of the huge investments in nanotechnology, the translation of nanomedicines into clinical practice has been slow, due to several regulatory issues, including those addressing safety concerns, and also problems related to the maintenance of robustness and reproducibility in the up-scaling processes. On the other hand, several questions can be raised. Are nanostructures able to provide a better interaction with the cells? Do they warrant “smart” treatments that stimulate the body’s own repair mechanisms? Does this approach provide new solutions for a timely and improved diagnosis? Are preventive and personalized medicines becoming a reality? What will be the role of, for example, pharmacogenomics, in which the patient individual genes are targeted? The chapter also aims at providing an answer for all these challenges by focusing on the current status of representative nanomedicines still in pipeline or those that will be shortly or are already available in the market.

chapter.ce:title: Clinical applications of nanostructured drug delivery systems
chapter.ce:subtitle: From basic research to translational medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotechnology has become a driving force for innovation in medicine and modern healthcare, boosting advances in therapeutics, biosensors, and imaging systems. This is evidenced by the number of studies encompassing clinical applications of nanostructure-based formulations, which has been increasingly expanding. Cancer, central nervous system, and immune disorders are the leading areas in the research and development of new and better medicines, along with an early diagnosis. This chapter presents an overview of the clinical applications of nanosystems, ranging from basic research to translational medicine. In spite of the huge investments in nanotechnology, the translation of nanomedicines into clinical practice has been slow, due to several regulatory issues, including those addressing safety concerns, and also problems related to the maintenance of robustness and reproducibility in the up-scaling processes. On the other hand, several questions can be raised. Are nanostructures able to provide a better interaction with the cells? Do they warrant “smart” treatments that stimulate the body’s own repair mechanisms? Does this approach provide new solutions for a timely and improved diagnosis? Are preventive and personalized medicines becoming a reality? What will be the role of, for example, pharmacogenomics, in which the patient individual genes are targeted? The chapter also aims at providing an answer for all these challenges by focusing on the current status of representative nanomedicines still in pipeline or those that will be shortly or are already available in the market.

ce:section.ce:para: Depending on the purpose, whether for diagnosis, imaging, or therapy, different types of nanoparticles (NPs) have been developed, some of them used as multifunctional nanoplatforms, claiming the three applications at the same time. Nanostructured delivery systems (NDS) are complex nanosystems, which can be structurally divided into two parts, that is, the external layer (shell), which may be functionalized with a variety of small molecules, proteins, metal ions, and/or polymers, and the internal layer (core), which is essentially the central portion of the NPs and chemically composed of different materials (Fig. 4.1 ). The encapsulation of the components, such as drugs, proteins, or metals, is possible in the core and shell, and the respective distribution depends on their physicochemical characteristics and also on the type of nanocarrier. Functionalizing the particle surface allows for specific and unique applications of NPs that would otherwise be inaccessible.

NDS are attractive for medical applications, due to their unique characteristics, such as smaller size and, consequently, high specific surface area, great flexibility in terms of their chemical composition, and surface functionality by adsorption or cross-linking of proteins, drugs, and probes. All of these features should be taken into account to ensure a successful future clinical application. The composition of NPs and their engineering is variable, depending on the final purpose of the NP, i.e., it depends on the route of administration, the molecules to be transported, whether controlled release is intended, and so on. The first generation of NPs was composed by a basic matrix, with small molecules entrapped or simply adsorbed onto their surface. However, as with any external particles that enter the body, NDS face multiple defense systems aimed at recognizing, neutralizing, and eliminating foreign substances, therefore limiting the therapeutic approach. These defense mechanisms include the reticulo-endothelial system (RES), opsonization, and immunogenicity [1,2]. First-generation NPs accumulate in and passively target the liver and spleen. This led to the introduction of the second-generation NPs, developed with a hydrophilic polymer coating, to protect the NPs from the defense mechanisms, by inhibiting the hydrophobic protein–particles interactions. Consequently, residence time of the particles in the bloodstream was increased, with avoidance of the RES. The third-generation of NPs was designed intending to actively target cells.

It is feasible to promote NPs affinity to a specific tissue by decorating their surface with targeting molecules. The target will be something that does not exist in normal cells, such as surface receptors that are overexpressed in cancer cells. The engineering of NDS has to be built not only to make them invisible to the body defense mechanisms but also to improve the pharmacokinetics of available drugs, reducing drug biodistribution to nontarget compartments, and overcoming solubility and stability issues. Also, these NDS protect drugs from rapid metabolization and clearance, improving stability and treatment specificity. Due to their ability to interact with the cells and the compartments, NDS can be engineered to respond to endogenous and/or exogenous stimuli (Fig. 4.1). The endogenous triggers, such as pH variations, enzymes, glucose, or redox gradient, can be used depending on the pathological characteristics of the disease. In turn, the exogenous triggers comprise external force, including temperature, magnetic field, ultrasound, light, electric pulse, or strong radiation. Lastly, fourth-generation NPs have multifunctional capabilities, which enable advances in early detection, diagnostics, and therapeutic strategies. Moreover, the engineering of NDS facilitates the development of multifunctional systems for targeted drug delivery (TDD), combined therapies, diagnostic applications or theranostics [3]. The theranostic approach is based on the co-delivery of therapeutic and imaging agents in a single NDS [4]. It allows a noninvasive, real-time visualization of a drug’s whereabouts, including drug pharmacokinetic and biodistribution profiles. Additionally, these tools enable clinicians to address the intra- and interpatient heterogeneity of disease, also pointing to a future application in personalized medicine. Multifunctional core-shell NDS can perform a significant role in the near future, suggesting new prospects for monitoring the response to therapy in real time. Thus, current nanotechnologies prevent drug release before reaching the target tissues or organs, allowing delivery of drugs at desired rate in the sites of action, either by an active or passive targeting strategy or triggered mechanism [5].

Nanotechnology is becoming increasingly recognized, with several examples of first-generation systems approved by regulatory entities, such as Doxyl, Myocet, DaunoXome, DepoCyt, Marqibo, Mepact, DaunoXome, Abraxane, and NanoTherm/MagForce. Although the number of NDS has significantly increased in the market, the translation into clinically viable therapies is still a challenge. First, it is necessary to establish the preclinical proof-of-concept for the efficacy and safety of a novel NDS product, which will be subsequently submitted to clinical trials (Fig. 4.2 ). However, the complexity in the scale-up process, and lack of correlation between preclinical and clinical studies, results in problems for obtaining regulatory and ethical approval. The engineering and control over the biodistribution are some of the current obstacles to translating nanotechnologies to therapies.

In this chapter, NPs with the most promising clinical potential, the status of current several clinical trials and those approved are described. The main advances in NPs, their revolutionary capabilities over the past decades, as a drug delivery and diagnostic system are emphasized. Recent researches, involving both in vitro and in vivo studies are analyzed. Although NDSs can be broadly used in several diseases, this review is mainly focused on cancer, central nervous systems (CNS), and immune diseases for the future clinical translation of NDS. Biodistribution, pharmacokinetic parameters, and toxicity will be addressed. The importance of NDS in personalized medicine and the difficulties found for the respective clinical translation will be also discussed.

A variety of NDS have been developed for different applications, and depending on the nature of their composition, they can be divided in two major groups: organic and inorganic, or a combination of both [6,7]. Organic NPs, including liposomes (LPs), polymeric NPs, lipid NPs, micelles/polymeric micelles (PMs), protein-based NPs, and inorganic NPs, such as iron oxide NPs (IONPs), mesoporous silica NPs (MSNPs), gold NPs (AuNPs), and quantum dots (QDs) are being developed for various applications, such as drug delivery or diagnostic and theranostic (Table 4.1 ). Organic NPs are described as solid particles composed by organic compounds (essentially lipids, surfactants, or polymers). One of the most important characteristics of organic NPs is that they offer an easy way for the encapsulation of molecules. Additionally, the fact that the molecules used for the manufacturing of the organic NPs can be biodegradable makes them the most attractive systems for drug delivery and biomedical applications. Organic NPs differ theoretically from inorganic NPs in terms of the principles of fabrication [8].

Several inorganic NPs are currently used for biological applications, due to their capability of response to specific external stimuli, such as magnetic fields or near-infrared (NIR) light to enhance magnetic imaging or facilitate control drug release. Also, various inorganic NPs can be synthesized and modified in a way that facilitates incorporation of ligands or polymers to promote the enhancement of their biological function. Inorganic NPs have the ability to convert energy into heat through NIR light excitation or oscillating magnetic field stimulation, and also high ability for surface modifications, which enables targeting approaches for biologically active molecules [9]. The main advantages of inorganic NPs is their robustness, resistance to enzymatic degradation, and interesting intrinsic properties, such as optical and superparamagnetic properties as well as some biological activities, e.g., protein transport facilitation into cells. These different capabilities depend on the chemical composition of the core/shell. The toxicity associated to the presence of the heavy metal is minimized with the use of biocompatible coatings by surface modification. Although inorganic NPs have not been approved for any drug delivery application, they are widely investigated for diagnostic applications, using their distinctive abilities to respond to external stimuli and local physical changes
[10]. However, the native toxicity and nondegradability have also been addressed as main concerns for future clinical translations of these NPs, which limits the development of inorganic NPs as theranostic nanomedicine [11]. A new approach consists of combining organic NPs with inorganic NPs and uses the advantages of both in a unique formulation, essentially directed at theranostics. This combination of different functions can complement and improve current therapies.

Bilayer phospholipid systems, called LPs, were described in 1965 [12]. LPs are defined as phospholipid vesicles consisting of one or more concentric lipid bilayers enclosing discrete aqueous spaces. Due to this architecture, LPs are capable of encapsulating both hydrophobic and hydrophilic drugs [13,14]. The first liposomal surface modification was introduced in the late 1980s by coating with a hydrophilic polymer, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [15,16]. It was reported that the conventional structure of LPs increased interaction with body components, and modifications on the LP architecture was needed [17]. The new LP type, termed stealth LPs, led to an improved stability and enhanced circulation time in the blood. Further surface modification strategies, such as stimuli-responsive, cationic, and ligand-based, allowed to improve the therapeutic efficacy, reduce toxicity, and increase the stability of the nanosystem. All these improvements led to numerous preclinical and clinical trials in diverse areas, including the delivery of anticancer, antifungal, and antibiotic drugs, gene therapy, anesthetics, and antiinflammatory drugs [18–25].

The application of multiapproaches in a single LP type has been described in the literature, and some examples will be mentioned. A targeting-temperature-triggered liposomal NP, with incorporation of cyclic arginine–glycine–aspartic acid (cRGD) and elastin-like polypeptide, respectively, was described [26]. The in vivo tumoral accumulation showed that the tumor-targeting was fivefold higher than that of the nontargeting LPs (Fig. 4.3 ) [26]. The LP was stable in physiological conditions (at 37°C), and the release of doxorubicin (DOX), placed in the LP core, occurred in mild hyperthermia at 42°C. Another type of stimuli-response is photoionization. This type of stimuli was used and achieved, leading to photo-triggered LPs, by using a copolymer of poly(vinyl alcohol) bearing a malachite green moiety (PVAMG) [27]. Under irradiated conditions, the copolymer caused morphological changes in the membrane without completely disrupting the LP. However, in this research, only the feasibility and performance were evaluated. It is also planned to investigate the toxicity of PVAMG under dark and irradiated conditions, so as to support the feasibility of its use as a drug carrier. There are studies that use dual stimuli-response conjugated as targeting approach. For example, dual signal-sensitive LPs may provide both temperature and pH sensitivity [28]. The LPs not only showed content release under acidic pH (≈5.5) and elevated temperature conditions but also a strong affinity onto HeLa cells. This approach allowed to overcome the lack of accuracy of LPs to release the drugs, specifically in the endosome or lysosome interiors of the cells in the targeting tissues. Aptamosome, an RNA aptamer-conjugated LP, is a new approach for gene therapy, associated with the LPs to target prostate epithelial cells [29]. The results displayed a significantly higher toxicity associated to the targeted LPs than to nontargeted ones, as well as a selective retention in the tumor tissue. As such, the encapsulation of drugs with aptamer-conjugated LPs will support the use of these bioconjugates in clinical practice. An important application of LPs focused in Alzheimer’s disease (AD), for which a multifunctional LP was developed [30], relying on the nanocarrier sequestering Abeta42 in human biological fluids. The LPs were used as a therapeutic strategy based on the reduction of Abeta toxicity. Other reports have described the use of magnetic NP for detection of AD biomarkers [31,32].

Although LPs have been used as drug delivery systems, they also play an important role in molecular imaging, using a variety of synthetic phospholipids with functional groups allowing for stimuli-response properties in sensing applications [33]. The role of LPs in imaging is to provide selective targeting either to normal or pathological tissue and to be retained long enough to allow a satisfactory signal. These formulations have the ability for the incorporation of new imaging agents, such as Gd3+, 64Cu, and 18F, for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and nuclear imaging [34]. Moreover, in comparison to free contrast agents, which have short plasma half-life, the encapsulation of contrast agents allowed a life span in the tissue for several hours. In this regard, a number of studies have been carried out on the relevant nanocarriers. Thus, new possibilities in the design of advanced image-guided therapeutics have been explored. In 2012, a theranostic LP, coated with folate for receptor targeting and co-delivery of docetaxel and QDs was developed. The folate receptor (FR) targeted LPs showed significantly higher cellular uptake and, consequently, higher cytotoxicity. Also, the half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) was assessed and showed a higher effectiveness to FR targeted LPs than the commercial reference (Taxotere) [35]. A comparison between cationic, PEGylated, and deformable LPs for bioimaging and therapeutic delivery was also conducted [36]. It was concluded that the cationic LPs demonstrated higher cytotoxicity, cell migration, and significantly increased fluorescence signal compared to other carriers, and thus promising for a theranostics approach. Recently, a targeted LP was developed in an optoacoustic contrast platform [37]. In this, indocyanine green, a photo-absorbent/fluorescent probe, and an anti-MUC-1 “humanized” monoclonal antibody were incorporated. Targeted LPs proved to be effective for the improvement of cellular uptake, cytotoxicity, and the intratumoral liposomal distribution. Gold nanoclusters were used in a targeting approach for breast cancer cell detection, within a robust sensing LP for breast cancer diagnosis and symptomatic treatment [38]. The targeting ability of ErbB2/Her2 antibody provided high sensitivity with high selectivity of breast cancer cells; also, the detection sensitivity was significantly enhanced. It was possible to confirm the versatility of LPs, including clinical diagnostics, bioanalysis, and bionanotechnology. Multifunctional LPs as platform for imaging and drug delivery were also prepared based on coated LP with amphiphilic hyaluronic acid (HA)–ceramide and Magnevist, a contrast agent for MRI. Cellular uptake of the drug from the nanohybrid LP was enhanced due to targeting approach and in vivo contrast was higher. This type of LP may be a useful candidate for real-time cancer diagnosis and therapy [39].

Another liposomal application is in the biosensors field. LPs are good signal transducers due to their large surface area and encapsulation volume. So, they can entrap large amounts of signaling molecules across a wide spectrum of sensing modalities and amplify the signal intensity [40].

Micelles are colloidal dispersions, consisting of amphiphilic molecules that self-assemble to form nanocapsules in water. Amphiphilic molecules consist of a hydrophobic and a hydrophilic moiety, which self-assemble in water above a critical micelle concentration in a thermodynamic process. Several micelles, after contact with blood and tissue components have low reactivity, enabling them to remain in circulation for long periods of time without being recognized by proteins and/or phagocytic cells. However, when applied in vivo, conventional micellar nanomedicines face difficulties, due to their premature disintegration in systemic circulation.

PMs share the same principle of micelles, having, however, in their composition amphiphilic block copolymers. These can self-assemble into well-defined core-shell structures [62] via cross-linking, which allows them to overcome the premature disintegration. Thus, there is an improvement of drug encapsulation, prolonged circulation times, enhanced stability, and control over the rate of drug release. Moreover, the flexible polymer chemistry has provided a potential for targeted and stimuli responsive delivery or diagnostic. Polymer flexibility allows integration of targeting ligands, triggered release, and the delivery of imaging agents.

There are many researches in the therapeutic and diagnostic applications of micelles/PMs [63–66]. To improve the stealth property of PEG, an α-glutamyl group was conjugated with PEG and PMs with α-glutamyl-terminated PEG shells were prepared [67]. After incubation with bovine serum albumin, the size of the PMs only slightly changed. However, the PMs size with α-glutamyl terminated group decreased markedly. Thus, the addition of α-glutamyl group not only showed low nonspecific protein adsorption but also exhibited a longer in vivo blood circulation time when compared with other micelles without α-glutamyl groups. The better stealth of the micelles provided by the α-glutamyl group was attributed to the zwitterionic property of the α-glutamyl groups [67]. The synthesis of a new HA conjugate with co-poly(styrene maleic acid) to constitute a micelle has also been reported [68]. The results showed a better cellular internalization and a higher anticancer response, due to the targeting approach. Another study reported the design of a micellar formulation that combined QDs and an anticancer drug (DOX) to build a robust theranostic platform (≈50nm) [69]. It was observed a sustained release of DOX, a strong time-dependent uptake of the micelles, a biocompatible and strong potential. Multifunctional unimolecular micelles based on a novel brush-shaped amphiphilic block copolymer have been developed and evaluated [70]. The cellular uptake was proved to be much higher using those micelles than with the nontargeted ones. Also, a higher level of tumor accumulation was demonstrated when compared with the nontargeted ones, based on the PET imaging and ex vivo biodistribution studies. The design of PMs to simultaneously address MRI and treatment of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) was described. A multifunctional polymer triblock copolymer poly(lactic acid)–poly(ethylene glycol; PEG)–poly(L-lysine), linked the MRI contrast agent and also provided a targeting approach. The PMs showed not only a significant higher T1 relaxivity in vitro compared to the reference but also a higher antitumor efficacy [71]. Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs), as an MRI contrast agent and busulfan, as a model for lipophilic antineoplastic drugs, were encapsulated into poly(ethylene glycol)–co-poly(caprolactone) (PEG-PCL) micelles [72]. SPION loaded-PEG-PCL micelles showed contrast enhancement in MRI, the in vitro cellular uptake of micelles was time-dependent, and the biodistribution studies revealed a high concentration into the lungs, followed by redistribution and accumulation in liver and spleen. The preclinical biodistribution studies of these micelles establish a basis for the potential use of these carriers for theranostic application. In another study, a triblock copolymer, comprising a hydroxamic acid functionalized center block that chelated iron was synthesized to form a stabilized micelle. The iron-imparted stability significantly improved the integrity of the micelle, extended pharmacokinetics, and improved the paramagnetic properties, exhibiting contrast in the tumors for imaging by magnetic resonance [73]. The development of an in situ formed pH-responsive unimolecular micelle–AuNP was proposed by Lin et al. [74]. These nanocarriers were developed as a potential theranostic agent at the full advantage of dual-functionalization for computed tomography (CT) imaging and drug delivery. The results revealed good stability, high drug entrapment efficiency (EE), as well as in vitro pH-controlled drug release and tumor accumulation properties. Finally, this theranostic approach exhibited superior anticancer efficacy and effective CT imaging abilities under both in vitro and in vivo conditions [74].

Thus, due to the versatility of drug/contrast agent delivery based on micelles, a large number of promising preclinical studies addressing numerous approaches to optimize these nanomedicines have arisen. In 1989, the first therapeutic efficacy of a drug using PMs was described, and since then several formulations have been developed and entered in clinical trials (Table 4.2). The objective of the use of the PMs is, essentially, in the cancer treatment [75]. The ability of PMs to solubilize poorly soluble drugs and circumvent multidrug resistance is the main reasons for this fairly efficient clinical translation. NC-6300 is a pH-responsive PM that can release epirubicin in acidic conditions of intratumoral microenvironment or the endo-/lysosomal compartment in cancer cells. This nanocarrier allows an increase in antitumor effect and reduces cardiotoxicity of the free epirubicin. There are clinical trials in progress to study the highest tolerated dose of NC-6300 that can be given to patients with advanced solid tumors or soft tissue sarcoma [52].

The translation of these complex NPs to clinical application is very slow. The preparation of these nanosystems requires complicated procedures, and their scalability and implementation in the industry is difficult. The number of PM products introduced in the market did not increase since, despite the great preclinical success, in terms of improved efficacy and reduced toxicity, a better in vivo performance would be expected. They only showed moderate improvement in patients in terms of pharmacokinetics, efficacy, and tolerability [76]. On the other hand, the targeting approach of PM formulations led to a reduced accumulation in off-targeting tissues and a higher accumulation in the site of action.

Lipid nanoparticles (LNs) have a special interest, because they gather advantages and minimize limitations associated with the conventional colloidal systems, such as high physical and chemical stability, avoiding problems associated with sterilization procedures, easy scale-up production, use of low-cost raw materials, and absence or reduction of acute or chronic toxicity, because they are composed by excipients with accepted status by the regulatory authorities (e.g., GRAS status—generally regarded as safe). In addition, LNs have a solid matrix at both room and body temperature, allowing a controlled release and chemical protection of the drug. Therefore, the therapeutic efficiency is improved, as a result of the modulation in release combined with a better tolerability and targeting of the encapsulated drug. The reduced particle size, along with a high specific surface area, the biocompatible, and biodegradable nature and release modulation properties make the LNs an attractive nanocarrier for drug delivery [77–79].

The therapeutic potential of LNs can be further expanded by the presence of the suitable ligands on their surface [80,81]. This can be accomplished via decorating the drug carrier with specific molecules, which will result in higher retention at target sites and augmented uptake of the drugs by cells. For this purpose, a polymer–lipid hybrid formulation consisting of solid lipid nanoparticles (SLN) as core and chitosan (CH)– HA as a shell on the periphery was developed [82]. HA was used on the outermost layer due to its ability to bind to CD44 receptors, which are overexpressed in many cancer cells. The results showed that HA conjugation with the SLN increased the intracellular concentration after 24hours (≈73.8% ± 2.8%), due to ligand–receptor interactions. In this preclinical study, a coated paclitaxel-LN delivery system was successfully formulated and significantly affected the receptor-mediated binding and internalization of the drug delivery system into the cells. Another group assessed the ability of conjugation of a monoclonal melanotransferrin antibody and tamoxifen on the surface of etoposide-entrapped SLN to targeting the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and glioblastoma multiforme (GBM). The results determined an enhanced BBB permeability coefficient when compared with SLN without the targeting approach. Thus, the functionalization of surface with tamoxifen and monoclonal melanotransferrin antibody can be promising in delivering GBM drugs for future clinical application. Nanostructured lipid carrier (NLC)–based formulations of diindolylmethane derivatives, to improve the oral bioavailability and anticancer effects in triple negative breast cancer, showed a significant increase in the oral bioavailability and anticancer activity in animal models [83]. Thus, the use of NLC made possible by reducing the dose of the free drugs by almost one third. Other authors developed and optimized a chitosan-coated nanostructured lipid carrier (CH-NLC) formulation, with the capacity to reach the brain after intranasal administration [84]. Mucoadhesive and positively charged NPs were produced, with a particle size of approximately 100nm, promoting the delivery of drugs to the brain after intranasal administration. In vivo studies carried out in C57 mice evidenced that although a low percentage of CH-NLCs reached the brain; this amount could be sufficient to exert biological activity when a specific drug was encapsulated. Moreover, it was reported in the literature that the drug concentration in the brain after the intranasal administration of loaded chitosan NPs was significantly higher than the concentration detected after intravenous drug administration. A study involving folate-decorated FA-NLC loaded with cisplatin (DDP) and paclitaxel (PTX) for the treatment of head and neck cancer revealed that the tumor regression was clearly observed in the FA-DDP/PTX NLCs group; the tumor growth was prominently delayed (182mm3), whereas in the saline-treated group, tumor volume grew rapidly to 963mm3 [85]. The tumor growth inhibition ratio with folate-decorated NLC was higher (81.1%) than that one obtained with the undecorated NLCs. Another interesting approach investigated the influence of the decoration order, pre- or post-decoration, as critical variable for the performance of NPs [86]. The authors found some differences in the particle size, i.e., for post-bombesin decorated DOX and DNA NLC (post-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC) was 128nm, while for pre-bombesin decorated DOX and DNA NLC (Pre-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC) was 101nm, although the DNA and DOX EEs were similar. Another difference was founded in in vivo antitumor activity. The results showed that post-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC had a higher efficacy in inhibiting the tumor growth than pre-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC. Thus, although both decorated NLC formulations have shown better transfection and antitumor effect than undecorated NLC in vitro and in vivo, post-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC revealed significantly better performance when compared to pre-BN-DOX-DNA-NLC. Another study used NLC for the combination therapy, including DOX and vincristine (VCR), and proved to be more in vitro and in vivo effective than the equivalent dose of free single drug formulation. It also significantly reduced the drug-associated systemic toxicity, with DOX/VCR NLCs displaying the lowest IC50 (0.26mg/mL), when DOX to VCR ratio was 1:1. DOX/VCR NLCs demonstrated the highest antitumor activity and a significant synergistic in vitro and in vivo effect. Thus, it made possible to overcome the drug resistance associated to single drug administration and reduce the systemic toxicity [87].

Also, LNs have the ability to incorporate and release genetic material [88]. A study reported the surface modification of NLC as multifunctional nanomedicine for co-delivery of enhanced green fluorescence protein plasmid (pEGFP) and DOX. Transferrin-modified DOX and pEGFP co-encapsulated NLC (T-NLC) exhibited a particle size of 198nm and a surface charge of +19mV. T-NLC displayed remarkably greater gene transfection efficiency, an efficient targeting approach, and an enhanced antitumor activity [89]. Another group working in the development and synthesis of multifunctional NLC-based system for efficient delivery of anticancer drugs and siRNA directly to the lungs by inhalation observed an enhanced antitumor activity along with the tumor-targeted local delivery prompted by the proposed NLC system [90]. Another study corroborates the idea that NLCs are good vehicles to release genetic material [91]. Accordingly, the NLCs had a particle size of 157nm, and 82% of gene loading quantity and the results displayed remarkably higher transfection efficiency than nonmodified NLCs, both in vitro and in vivo.

LNs have also shown a higher contrast agent payload and a good accessibility of the paramagnetic metal ions to the bulk water molecules [92]. It means that lower doses of the paramagnetic metal ions can be foreseen for attaining the same image quality in clinical use. In vivo targeting and biodistribution of paramagnetic SLN for MRI has been described [93]. In this study, the amount of the loaded amphiphilic gadolinium (Gd3+) complex was about 20% considering the total phospholipid content, resulting in a huge payloaded metal ion per NP. Other study also described SLN with NMR relaxometric properties [94]. The relaxivity properties of the investigated SPION SLN were found to be better than those of SPION-based contrast agents commonly considered as standard in the literature. The development of a magnetic SLNs formulation with iron oxide cores embedded within a glyceryl trimyristate solid matrix was assessed regarding the hematocompability and their cytotoxicity performance in human HT29 colon adenocarcinoma cells [95]. The results displayed a potential candidate for theranostic applications. Kuang et al. studied the behavior of hybrid SLN with a specific targeting with cRGD and loaded with contrast agent. The cellular uptake studies evidenced fluorescence in the cytoplasm when the cells were incubated with cRGD-IR-780 SLN (Fig. 4.4 ). This result suggests that c(RGDyK) decorated on the surface of SLN could effectively enhance the cellular uptake of NPs by U87MG cells. The in vitro photothermal toxicity displayed a decrease in cell viability, with the cRGD-NPs combined with laser radiation the treatment that more effectively kill the tumor cells (≈15.1%). Moreover, the use of cRGD-IR-780 SLN demonstrated effective accumulation in tumor in vivo (≈3.57×109 photons/sec/mm2). The higher accumulation of SLN in the tumor when compared with kidneys and lungs was ascribed to the active targeting. The group developed and clearly demonstrated that cRGD-IR-780 SLN is a versatile theranostic agent and safe for NIR-imaging guided photothermal therapy (PTT) [96]. Grillone et al. developed a sorafenib-loaded magnetic solid lipid nanoparticles (Sor-Mag-SLN) able to enhance the drug delivery to the diseased site. The study demonstrated that Sor-Mag-SLN had an optimal morphological stability with a regular spherical shape and size (<300nm) and were able to inhibit HepG2 cancer cell proliferation due to the sorafenib cytotoxic effect.

LNs with a potential dual function, as both negative contrast and therapeutic agent, are a promising theranostic tool for the treatment of cancer, and future experiments will be carried out to study the performance in vivo of the proposed nanocarrier. The preparation and evaluation of NLC consisting of QDs and drug (camptothecin) formulation has been performed [97]. Different QDs were selected, with carboxyl-function QDs or PEG QDs, loaded into NLC to compare bioimaging efficiencies at both in vitro and in vivo studies. The QD incorporation slightly increased the NPs diameter (221–245nm). QDNLC with carboxyl derivatization seemed to have been the best performance ensuring high cytotoxicity, and significant fluorescence labeling of cells and solid tumors. Patel et al. described multifunctional lipid NPs (<200nm), considering a targeting delivery approach using PEG and vascular endothelial growth factor peptides, CREKA (Cys-Arg-Glu-Lys-Ala), and conjugated DIM-CpPhC6H5 (DIM-P) for the treatment of lung cancer [98]. The results showed that the targeting approach increased 3-fold the binding to clotted plasma proteins in tumor vasculature, when compared to unconjugated NLC; at the same time, in vivo optical and ultrasound imaging studies displayed a greater movement of CREKA peptide–conjugated NLC in tumor vasculature (≈40-fold), when compared to peptide unconjugated NLC.

The commercial possibility of any delivery system is managed by the capability of a large-scale production method, yielding a product of a quality that is approved by the regulatory authorities, and the cost of the materials. Considering this scenario, LNs are easily available, also involving low-cost raw materials, when compared with LPs or PMs. For this reason, many efforts have been made for developing and investigating LNs, and there are also various patents available about this type of nanocarriers [99,100]. However, the transition from the basic research to the clinical application needs more industrially driven studies, mainly in the therapeutic and diagnostic fields. The LNs in the market belong to the cosmetic field. The first cosmetic product entering into the market was Cutanova Nanorepair Q10 cream, in October 2005. Other LNs in the market are described in Table 4.2. The research examples described earlier through different routes and for different applications, therapeutic, and/or diagnosis clearly illustrate the promise of these NPs for the pharmaceutical market and describes the exciting improvement in this technology in the last decade [101]. Considering the increasing patents concerning LNs-based formulations, it can be expected that the number of clinical trials pertaining to NLC will considerably increase in the future. Briefly, NLC appears to be one step closer to their translation into the clinics [102]. However, more preclinical and clinical studies should be implemented in a near future to introduce these formulations in the pharmaceutical market.

Polymeric NPs (PNPs), PMs (as described before), dendrimers, and polymer conjugates have been explored for targeted delivery of therapeutic moieties in different diseases. PNPs are one of the most studied organic strategies for nanomedicine, due to their great flexibility in tailoring their chemical composition, size, morphology, biodegradability, and surface functionalization. They are obtained from synthetic polymers, such as chitosan, polyamines, polyesters, poly(amino esters), polyanhydrides, among others. These NPs showed many benefits compared to other NPs, particularly in the transport of drugs, because their physicochemical properties can be more easily controlled, and the polymer shell provides protection to the drug against the degradation activities of body enzymes. In other words, the drug/contrast agents release can be attractively tailored via controlled polymer biodegradation or appropriate stimulus activation [103]. Stimuli activation mechanisms of drug release range from passive to active [104–109]. The molecules can be transported in the inner core of NPs (nanocapsules), within the NP polymeric matrix or onto the NP surface (nanospheres). They possess a great capability to take on an extensive range of applications with high efficient reproducibility, in therapeutics and/or diagnosis application, and at the same time it is possible to support surface functionalization [110].

So, in the literature there are some studies with ligand-PNPs conjugation for TDD, ligand-functionalized polymers for targeted NP self-assembly, drug-functionalized polymers for combinational drug therapy, and theranostic NPs by combined imaging and therapy. Sarvagalla et al. described therapeutic PNPs loaded with AZD1152-hqpa (aurora kinase inhibitor) [111]. PNPs containing hydrophobic acid significantly improved the pharmacokinetic profiles of the drug AZD1152-hqpa, as compared with the control agent (AZD1152). Drug efficacy and tolerability were also improved, and toxicity decreased in in vivo animal experiments, resulting in a better therapeutic index for the nanoformulation. Hence, AZD1152-hqpa could be tested in the clinic at a dose level similar to, or higher than that one used for AZD1152, with lower incidence of toxicity. Zhu et al. developed PNPs that used protein ligands on the particle surface, while simultaneously carrying and delivering therapeutic proteins [112]. In fact, the results showed that the proteins could be released in a controlled manner with diversified release kinetics patterns, and the surface-loaded protein could enhance targeting strategy.

Thus, various disease-targeting ligands can be bound to NPs by chemical and/or physical methods, and both therapeutic compounds and imaging contrast agents can be loaded onto the NPs for theranostic applications, which may provide a potential delivery system for tailor-made, personalized medicine.

It can be envisioned that with the validation of NPs products currently in clinical development, an increasing number of novel NP technologies (e.g., combination therapeutic and theranostic NPs) will emerge for bench to bedside translation (Table 4.2). CALAA-01 is a PNP formulation used for a siRNA delivery [118]. The preclinical toxicity studies showed that one or more of the delivery components within CALAA-01, rather than the siRNA component, were primarily responsible for the adverse effects observed. CALAA-01 is a targeted, polymer-based NP containing small interfering RNA (siRNA) and, to our knowledge, was the first RNA interference (RNAi)–based, experimental therapeutic to be administered to cancer patients. BIND-014 is a polymeric NP containing docetaxel that concentrates in the neovasculature surrounding tumors and targets prostate-specific membrane antigen. The enhanced delivery of bioactive molecules through the targeted delivery by means of a nanocapsule provides numerous opportunities for the research and future development of novel improved therapies. However, it is very challenging to design systems that combine ease of preparation, and low expense with biocompatibility and optimal therapeutic efficiency. Furthermore, thorough in vivo trials should be carried out to evaluate the pharmacokinetic and the biodistribution profile of PNPs to achieve an optimal treatment success. The final destination of such systems is the immune system (IS) cells, so there is a need to carefully address this issue. In general, there is a strong need to design robust, indirect, and assay-based methods and imaging modalities to monitor the fate of these TDDs, particularly to understand their interactions after cellular uptake by phagocytes in the human body. The high production cost and low investment in the fabrication of multifunctional TDDs are key factors limiting their widespread application [119]. The above-mentioned failures need to be wisely addressed by choosing cost-effective fabrication and scale-up procedures.

Among biodegradable NPs, protein-based nanoparticles (ProNPs) have received wide interest because of their biodegradability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, advantages of absorbability, colloidal stability, self-assembling properties, flexibility to surface modifications to allow attachment of drugs or contrast agents and targeting ligands, and their ease to scale up during manufacture, over other NDDs [120]. It is believed that the conjugated products have the ability to maintain the advantages of individual components. So, they are easy to produce with controllable size distribution, able to improve the solubility of drugs, have a low cost and enhanced circulation time in vivo. Most proteins are self-assembling molecules, with an amphiphilic structure, including both hydrophobic and hydrophilic domains. The hydrophilic portion can be charged or uncharged. The morphology of the final assembled structure is dependent not only on the structure of the monomer but also on the external environment in which self-assembly occurs. Considering the combination of these properties, systems based on proteins, including insoluble proteins (e.g., zein and gliadin) and water-soluble proteins (e.g., bovine and human serum albumin), have shown potential therapeutic and/or diagnostic applications.

Albumin is a natural noncovalent physiological protein, involved in the transport of molecules across endothelial barriers, having remarkable pharmacokinetics properties owing to its long half-life time in the bloodstream. The advantages associated with the structure of albumin imparts it with the ability to interact with a variety of molecules, while the functional groups present on their surface allow a large number of modifications, making it an ideal nanocarrier system [121]. Also, the ability of accumulation in solid tumors and inflamed tissue is the crucial point for developing albumin-based drug delivery systems for tumor targeting. Besides, these types of NPs can also be used as efficient high-loading drug carriers, consequently enabling the incorporation of significant amount of drug substances into NPs matrix, which is crucial for market-available nano-drugs. The properties of albumin, such as its high solubility at pH 7.4, stability in the pH range of 4 to 9, ability to support temperatures up to 60°C without any deleterious effects, make it an attractive macromolecular carrier capable of accommodating a wide variety of drugs [122,123]. Albumin-based NPs have been investigated as a potential carrier for the delivery of numerous molecules, including drugs, contrast agents, genes, and peptides [124–130]. Kim et al. developed and evaluated albumin-based NPs with a new combined therapeutic approach that enables a reduced drug dose, thus limiting serious side effects. Albumin-based NPs were produced by high pressure homogenization, encapsulating two anticancer drugs, curcumin and DOX, and the synergistic effects of these two drugs were showed [131]. However, there are avenues for the medical application of albumin that have not been fully explored, such as its use in photodynamic therapy, as a transport protein for metal complexes, combined therapeutical and diagnostics application [132]. Most of the contrast agents are hydrophobic and toxic, which limit their clinical applications. So, albumin-based NPs were used to load IR780 (HSA-IR780 NPs) by protein self-assembly (Fig. 4.8 ). In comparison with free IR-780, the solubility of HSA-IR780 NPs was 1000-fold increased, while the toxicity was decreased [133]. In vivo studies also showed a significant tumor inhibition by the injection of HSA-IR780 NPs into tumor-bearing mice. Yuan et al. developed albumin-based NPs with a spherical shape and particle size of 120.1 ± 26nm that corroborated the advantages of the use of ProNPs [134]. In vivo studies demonstrated that NPs-DOX were able to preferentially accumulate in tumor and showed great tumor inhibition. The cardiotoxicity induced by NPs-DOX was also considerably reduced. Another study reported the preparation of layer-by-layer albumin NPs coated with multilayer films, tumor necrosis factor-related apoptosis-inducing ligand (TRAIL) and biocompatible polysaccharide [135]. The results showed a stronger connection of the NPs in the presence of ligand. It is possible to find albumin-based NPs in the diagnostic field. For example, Flores et al. developed a protein coating of Gold-NPs (Au-NPs) based on a protein multilayer covering. Albumin-Au-NPs had a spherical shape and the presence of a low-density corona around the metal core defined the presence of the protein shell. The optical properties of Au-NPs were maintained even with albumin coating and also showed higher chemical stability [136]. For the first time, theranostic NPs combining mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNPs) and AuNCs encapsulating high amount of DOX and G drugs for pH-triggered autonomous multidrug delivery were developed. The strong fluorescence of the NPs was found almost exclusively in the tumor, and slightly in the liver and kidneys [137]. Another approach consisted in using nanocrystals with albumin coating, to increase the drug loading and the serum stability [138]. Curiously, albumin-nanocrystal remained more stable in serum than Abraxane, a commercial reference of albumin-based NPs formulation. Thus, albumin-nanocrystal showed higher antitumor efficacy than the commercial reference at the same dose.

Also, in the field of vaccines, ProNPs have been investigated and introduced in the market. One of the first examples of a self-associating protein particle was a protein extracted from the tobacco mosaic virus [139]. Indeed, antigen NPs arise as a leading strategy in the development of safe and potent vaccines, e.g., first exemplified by HBsAg. As mentioned before, there is a variety of natural proteins, and their configuration is structurally organized and stable, with a range size of 10–150nm, a size range optimal to interactions with the cells of the IS. Chimeric NPs can be obtained by self-assembly, or by covalent chemical attachment of an antigen to an NP [140]. Kanekiyo et al. have successfully designed ProNPs to present trimeric HA spike in its native conformation, with adequate spacing to ensure optimal access to potential broadly neutralizing antibodies directed to the stem [141]. The results showed ProNPs had the desired physical properties, enhanced the potency and breadth of neutralizing antibody responses, compared with the current commercial vaccine. Kanekiyo et al. created self-assembling NPs that showed different domains of gp350 in a symmetric array [142]. The structurally designed ProNPs vaccine increased neutralization, when compared to soluble gp350, by targeting a functionally conserved site of vulnerability, improving vaccine-induced protection in a mouse model. The specific immune responses and protective efficacy of protein NPs based on fusion proteins of flagellin carrier linked to conserved influenza epitopes were investigated by Deng et al. [143]. The fusion proteins engineering approach was used and allowed specific humoral immune responses and provided full protection against influenza A virus. There are two types of nanovaccinology: the therapeutic applied for cancer treatment [144], which treat other diseases or conditions, as Alzheimer [145], hypertension, or asthma; and prophylactic applied for the prevention of different diseases. Here, a computational approach may be useful to predict the interaction between protein–cell or protein–pathogen [146].

Currently in progress, several clinical trials are using albumin-bound NPs. So far, a variety of albumin-based NP approaches have been intensively researched for treatment, diagnosis, and personalized medicine, some of them with a completed way from laboratory to market. Abraxane (albumin-bound paclitaxel) was the first albumin-based NPs approved by FDA, as a potential candidate in the field of nanomedicine and cancer therapy. There are many ongoing trials examining the combination of Abraxane with other existing drugs to expand the approved indications for Abraxane (Table 4.2). Albumin-bound rapamycin (ABI-009) has been in a Phase 1 clinical trial to investigate the side effects and the best dose of NP albumin-bound rapamycin when given together with temozolomide and irinotecan hydrochloride in treating pediatric patients with solid tumors. Albumin-bound paclitaxel (ABI-007) has been in a clinical trial, in Phase 2. This trial will treat patients with advanced breast cancer with a new anticancer medicine, ABI-007, used in combination with two existing anticancer medications, carboplatin and herceptin [61]. Nab-paclitaxel in combination with biologic agents, such as antiangiogenics or HER2 targeted agents in the neoadjuvant setting, have shown the viability of their use in this treatment setting. However, with several of these trials still ongoing, it is difficult to draw definitive conclusions. Some vaccines containing ProNPs are in development and other ones in clinical applications. The researches’ focus is in hepatitis B virus, malaria, RSV, influenza, hepatitis E virus, HPV, Alzheimer, Ebola Virus, Type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, malignant melanoma, rabies, and human norovirus. RSV-F vaccine is a ProNPs vaccine used for respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). A Phase 2 randomized study aimed to evaluate the immunogenicity and safety of an RSV-F vaccine. The results showed that the vaccine appeared to be safe, immunogenic, and associated to reduced RSV infections [147,148]. For the treatment of prostate cancer, a human aspartyl-asparaginyl-β-hydroxylase–directed NP vaccine has been investigated for intradermal administration. Considering the potential application of NPs as vaccines, the different conformations would allow different epitope densities, which probably result in different immune responses. However, the application of NPs in vaccine delivery is still at an early stage of development. There are a number of challenges associated with these nanocarriers. Difficulty in reproducing synthesis, lack of knowledge of how physical properties of NPs affect their biodistribution and targeting, and how these properties influence their interactions with the biological system are some of the challenges. However, at present, a better understanding of the mechanisms of action of these vehicles and of the protein–drug interactions at the molecular level will provide a fundament for their further optimization to enable the design of ideal ProNPs and open more prospects for their use in the area of drug and gene delivery.

IONPs are magnetic particles particularly used as MRI contrast agents. They are classified in two groups, depending on their hydrodynamic particle size: superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPIO), with a particle size higher than 50nm, and ultrasmall superparamagnetic iron oxide particles, with a particle size less than 50nm. The quality and behavior of the IONPs depend on the synthetic methods, and their physicochemical properties, such as size, shape, and crystallinity. However, the naked IONPs have high chemical activity and are easily oxidized in air, generally resulting in loss of magnetism and dispersibility properties [149,150]. The surface of the IONPs may be positive or negative, depending upon the pH of the solution. At the isoelectric point (pH 6.8), the surface charge density is small and the particles are unstable in water and flocculate [151]. Thus, to improve biocompatibility in these media, it is necessary to utilize both electrostatic and steric stabilization. In this regard, the surface modification of iron oxide is crucial: with a large surface area, a high number of functional groups for targeting ligands can be attached and polymers can be chemically anchored or physically adsorbed on IONPs to form a single or double layer. The magnetic properties of IONPs can be used in numerous in vivo applications, as magnetic vectors that can be directed by means of a magnetic field gradient towards a certain location, such as in the case of TDD [152,153], magnetic contrast agents in MRI, and hyperthermia or thermoablation agents [154].

To protect the IONPs interactions from the physiological medium, while avoiding precipitation and agglomeration of particles, a targeting approach is used. IONPs-appropriate surface coatings divert IONPs towards various applications. Indeed, the coating is used not only to modulate their biocompatibility and improve the targeting but also to reduce their toxicity. Tran et al. [133] developed IONPs coated with oleic acid and bovine serum albumin for drug delivery. At the same time, oleic acid in the IONPs composition allowed encapsulation of hydrophobic drugs. However, further studies are required to evaluate the in vitro and in vivo performance for application in diagnostics [155]. Vismara et al. [156] described IONPs, with iron core and HA for diagnostics and targeting application, while BSA would promote stability, biocompatibility, and reduce toxicity. Despite the good results in terms of structural and morphological characterization, it is necessary to study the in vitro and in vivo performance. Computational analyses play a useful role in modeling the effects of different bodily environments in the interactions and can give information for the development of novel and exciting modes of targeted delivery of therapeutics or diagnostics to diseased tissues [157,158]. Martinez-Boubeta et al. [154] reported an experimental and theoretical analysis of magnetic hyperthermia. The single-domain cubic iron oxide particles had superior magnetic heating efficiency, compared to spherical particles of similar sizes. Also, Monte Carlo simulations supported the larger anisotropy of the cubic particles in comparison with the spherical particles [154]. A new strategy for cancer treatment is NIR laser-induced PTT. A variety of nanomaterials has been developed for this purpose. Zhou et al. developed PEGylated IONPs, which possessed triple functional properties in one entity: targeting, PTT, and imaging [159]. This new approach displayed an intrinsic high photothermal conversion efficiency comparable to gold nanorods and selective magnetic targeting. These characteristics have been converted into a high killing efficiency on tumors in in vitro and in vivo studies [159,160]. Shi et al. reported IONPs with theranostic functions and application in personalized medicine. IONPs were encapsulated with drug molecules and contrast agents, 64Cu-DOTA and Cy5.5, a triple active mode to make them available for positron emission tomography (PET), near-infrared fluorescence, and MRI (Fig. 4.9 ). The previously mentioned albumin coating had two main actions: promote a longer circulation half-life and increase the retention of the particles in the tumor, due to the more permeable vasculature environment associated to the tumor site [161]. The conjugation of IONPs and monoclonal antibodies was also studied [162]. Recently, the development of multifunctionalized IONPs that contain the anti-CD47 antibody and the chemotherapeutic drug gemcitabine in one formulation was reported. The combination has demonstrated good results in preclinical in vivo studies, and the developed platform supports a considerable potential for future therapeutic development. Nevertheless, before using these NPs in vivo, it is necessary to optimize the stability of the nanocarrier in terms of circulation and invisibility to the RES, so as to avoid premature clearance [163]. MRI is critical for visualizing soft tissue and organs, and about one-third of these techniques are contrast-enhanced MRI, mostly with gadolinium-based contrast agents (Gd). Although Gd is an approved contrast agent, some researches demonstrated the respective accumulation in organs. Thus, Wei et al. [164] developed zwitterion-coated exceedingly small superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (ZES-SPIONs) with pharmacokinetic and imaging properties comparable to other contrast agents in the market. They showed a high T1 contrast power, exhibited prolonged contrast, and determined the potential of ZES-SPIONs in preclinical MRI and magnetic resonance. The pH-responsive ligands were also reported using IONPs. This strategy is important in cancer treatment, once there are differences between the tumor environment and blood circulation [165]. Recently, it was reported that IONPs have the dual ability to act as magnetic and photothermal agents [166]. This dual action produced complete apoptosis-mediated cell death. While in vivo studies have shown that single-mode treatment reduced tumor growth, a dual-mode treatment caused complete tumor regression [167]. Additionally, in cancer therapy, the receptor-specific targeting may be used for detection of potential metastases at initial stages. Furthermore, results showed that the synergistic exploitation of the magnetic and photothermal properties of the NPs reduced the magnetic field and laser intensities that would be a prerequisite in the case both external stimuli were applied individually [168].

The biocompatibility and outstanding in vivo imaging characteristics have led to the extensive use of IONPs for diagnostics, imaging, and for decorating organic NPs as a theranostic application. The research and the clinical trials using IONPs are expanding due to their capabilities in diagnostics and treatment. Besides standard biocompatibility experimental approaches, this evolving transition from in vitro diagnostics to innovative in vivo therapeutics is complex. However, the challenge remains in the clinical translation of NP probes, and raises serious concerns about their pharmacokinetics, metabolism, and toxicity, in vivo and in vitro targeting efficiency, and long-term stability of the functionalized IONPs. The insufficiency of current clinical imaging techniques to quantify IONPs biodistribution at high concentrations has delayed current clinical applications of IONPs. Because early diagnosis and detection of specific diseases in vivo have pronounced implications in patient treatment and prognosis, more studies need to focus on the production of NP-based systems for imaging, while in parallel genomic and proteomic analysis can be used for identification and characterization of specific markers for clear differentiation of specific types. There are several clinical trials ongoing which use IONPs with various applications, such as multiple sclerosis, head and neck cancer, cancer of lymph node, lymphatic metastasis, liver cancer, pancreatic cancer, brain neoplasms, myocardial infarction, and renal transplant rejection (Table 4.2). Ferumoxytol, an example of approved IONPs in the clinic, has been well tolerated by patients, and its relative kidney-independent pharmacokinetics makes it a suitable alternative contrast agent to chelate gadolinium. The preclinical research using IONPs has promising results towards their application in the clinical fields, but only a few in vivo studies of IONPs have been successfully conducted in preclinical models [169]. However, the lack of toxicity, pharmacokinetics, and biodistribution studies on IONPs, and the absence of clinical benefits are the main barriers between lab reproducibility and clinical application. As reported in some studies, a significant number of SPIO-based NPs are sequestered by the liver and spleen. To avoid the negative biodistribution, a targeting approach is used [170,171]. Although these strategies increase the NP accumulation in tissues, a high fraction of the dose still accumulates in the liver [172–174]. Consequently, the toxicity of IONPs in the liver becomes a problem. Results in the literature are controversial, even if the toxicity depends on particle size, charge, and doses [175]. In an attempt to solve these problems, the complexity of IONPs increases. Thereafter, the manufacture of IONPs’ formulation limits their market entry, because of high cost and chemical synthesis complexity, as well as the successful co-encapsulation of both therapeutic and imaging agent. Finally, the combined strategies result in an increase of multimodal populations in the same formulation. Clinical success of the IONPs depends on the parameters discussed above.

MSNPs possess a robust, porous, domain, where molecules, such as drugs or imaging agents, can be stored. Thus, MSNPs have three key components: a solid support, a payload of cargo, and external machinery. The pore size and particle size can be finely adjusted in a precise wide range from 2 to 50nm and from 10nm to the micron range, respectively. Their properties allow a high stability for a wide range of chemical conditions. MSNPs may have different shapes and can be functionalized with stimulus-responsive components to yield mechanized silica NPs. In the literature, several kinds of stimuli have been used to release molecules from MSNPs, such as redox-activation [176], pH modification [177], and irradiation [178]. Despite being reported that MSNPs cannot be used for clinical application directly, due to the aggregation phenomena under physiological conditions, strategies involving surface modifications and response to stimuli could allow to overcome this issue. MSNPs are interesting delivery vehicles for drug and contrast agents, due to their large surface area, in vivo stability, and easily modifiable surface. The in vitro cellular tests and hemolytic experiments revealed the outstanding biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [179,180]. Surface modification of silica can improve in vivo circulation times, reduces toxicity, and increases drug adsorption/loading [181]. The surface of the MSNPs is enriched with silanol groups that are capable of reacting with several types of functional groups, such as targeting ligands, polymers, and fluorescent agents. Due to the ability to surface modification, it is possible to formulate a nanocarrier with different applications, such as biomedical imaging [182,183], therapeutic delivery [184], monitoring [182], and ablative therapies.

Targeted MSNPs based on pH- and redox-dual responsive tumor-triggered were also designed as drug carrier [185]. The ligand was affixed on the surface of the MSNPs via disulfide bonds, as a redox-responsive, as well as a tumor-targeting ligand. Peptide and monomethoxy PEG with benzoic-imine bond were considered as pH-sensitive components. The results showed that the NP did not act in normal tissues but demonstrated activity in tumor tissues. In the acidic pH tumor, the benzoic-imine bond cleavage and the redox signal glutathione in tumor cells induced rapid drug release intracellularly. However, the in vivo efficiency was not assessed [186]. In 2012, a research group reported the development and application of mesoporous silica based on triple-modal nanoprobes that possesses the long-term imaging ability to track tumor metastasis. MSNPs integrated multiple functions to simplify NIR optical, MRI, and PET imaging. The accumulation in the liver and spleen was confirmed by ex vivo biodistribution study. This indicated that the MSNPs probes are stable and slow to be cleared or metabolized in vivo. Thus, an optimization of particle size, ratios of imaging tags, and delivery route needs to be considered for potential clinical translation of mesoporous silica-based triple-modal nanoprobes [182]. In another study, a theranostic nanostructure with carbon and Si nanocrystals was encapsulated in the mesoporous silica NPs (CS-MSNPs) [184]. CS-MSNPs exhibited a high payload of insoluble drugs and a unique NIR-to-Vis luminescence imaging feature. To target the NPs, these were bioconjugated with a PEGylated phospholipid and HA (Fig. 4.10 ). The cell-TDD showed specifically targeting to overexpressing CD44 cancer cells, and NIR-to-vis luminescence imaging was confirmed. However, it is necessary to assess the toxicity and in vivo biodistribution of this promising theranostic platform [184]. Another research group evaluated the potential of albumin coated-MSNPs to overcome RES clearance in a mouse model of endometrioses. The results revealed that MSNPs labeled with 99mTc were mostly found in the intestine, spleen, blood, and bladder. MSNPs loaded with Avastin exhibited an increased accumulation in the spleen, blood, and liver. Human albumin MSNPs loaded with Avastin reduced the uptake, which was not significant in the spleen and liver [187]. Another group used surface modification in MSNPs to increase anticancer efficacy. For this purpose, carboplatin was loaded in the particles, promoting the presence of carboxylic acid (COOH) groups on the outer surface, and subsequently used to conjugate the amine group of the epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM). The targeting specificity of MSNPs resulted in an enhanced internalization for EpCAM-carboplatin-MSNPs compared to that of MSNPs. Also, EpCMSNPs exhibited a superior anticancer effect in retinoblastoma cells [188]. A tri-functional silica-based theranostic system was also developed [189]. The nanocarrier was loaded with CREKA (cancer-targeted), methotrexate (MTX, controlled drug delivery), and Cu-DTPA complex (PET imaging). The cell viability proved the potential of the NPs to be further studied in vivo. Moreover, the nanotheranostic system did not interfere with the action of MTX.

Depending on the fabrication method, it is possible to control particle size, porosity, crystallinity, surface charge and shape, and consequently the performance of MSNPs [190–192]. Thus, in 2009, scientists investigated how to control structural properties, such as pore geometry, connectivity (two- or three-dimensional) and pore wall chemistry, using MSNPs. It was clear that the connectivity (2D or 3D) of the pores was the key to control release rate with an increase of some orders of magnitude in diffusion coefficient from 3D porous [193]. Additionally, multifunctional dual-compartment MSNPs were described, with asymmetric nanocomposites showing a very uniform size of ~300nm and high surface area of ~1290m2/g. These MSNPs can be further applied into nanobiomedicine for heat and NIR light bimodal-triggered dual-drugs controllable release [194]. Although the shape of NPs plays an important role in defining biological effects, studies available on how shape contributes to the biological effect of heterogeneous nanomaterials are reduced. It was shown that long-rods possessed a higher intracellular internalization capability than the short-rod and sphere-like particles irrespective of the cells, due to the difference in the endocytosis pathways [195]. There were no significant shape effects on biocompatibility; however, the biodistribution of sphere-like particles took place with ease in the liver, while long-rod exhibited more retention in the spleen. The rod-like particles are favorably accumulated in tumor cells, which indicate an increased drug delivery efficacy in cancer therapy. The shape of the MSNPs was also assessed in oral administration. The increase in the aspect ratio of MSNPs promoted a decreased in vivo biodegradation, systematic absorption, and excretion, especially decreased through liver distribution and urinal excretion by oral administration [196]. The characterization of MSNPs for in vivo biodistribution was also proposed [197]. Biodistribution was carried out in healthy mice. The images demonstrated a high uptake by the liver, and a significant uptake in the lungs. Ya et al. have shown that MSNPs were taken up significantly by the liver and spleen [197]. The surface modification changed the behavior of MSNPs due to the increase of hydrodynamic size in circulation. Pharmacokinetic analysis exhibited a rapid clearance from systemic circulation; however, the modification of nonporous NPs influenced the distribution [198]. In general, the biodistribution is more influenced by NP porosity, surface characteristics, and less by geometry. The flexibility of MSNPs allows their use in personalized medicine. Characteristics, such as size, shape, porosity, and surface functionalization, affect how the particles behave under biological conditions and their therapeutic applicability. These characteristics influence release and interaction with biological components. Taken together these will influence safety, biodistribution, and therapeutic performance [199]. Unfortunately, in the literature there are not enough in vivo studies about the influence of each characteristic under biological conditions.

Biodistribution, toxicity, pharmacokinetic behavior, and bioavailability are crucial parameters that should be studied and discussed, to simplify the approval of these NPs into the market. As such, the manufacture of these NPs needs to focus on improved circulation, decreased toxicity, and targeted delivery, so as to build more efficacious and nontoxic therapeutic and diagnostic agents. Despite the progress in the biomedical applications of MSNPs, numerous challenges still need to be addressed before clinical success, including obtaining knowledge about all these parameters to facilitate the approval of these types of particles. A targeted silica NP formulation for image-guided intraoperative sentinel lymph node mapping in head and neck melanoma, breast, and gynecologic malignancies (Table 4.2) is currently in Phase 1. Therefore, thorough in vitro and in vivo studies are required to be conducted to outline the proper dose and type of MSNPs before clinical trials. Another critical question is connecting the gap between the successful in vitro experiments and challenging in vivo applications.

AuNPs consist of a gold (Au) core and surface coating on the shell. They are colloidal structures with diameters in the range of 1–150nm, and their size and shape are controllable [200]. AuNPs show tuned physical features, including optical properties, robustness, and high surface areas, making them perfect candidates for developing biomarker nanocarriers. Although the particle size determines the major part of AuNPs properties, it was also described that the shape influenced the optical properties. Not only the optical properties become crucial for the performance of AuNPs but also their biocompatibility and ease of surface modification. The surface of AuNPs can be personalized by ligand functionalization with selectively bound biomarkers to promote the specific targeting. Variation of these physicochemical properties can be simply accomplished by synthetic strategies, and they can produce AuNPs with advantages over conventional detection methods presently used in clinical application, as drug carrier [201], PTT, photoacoustic imaging, and surface-enhanced resonance spectroscopy [202–205]. The use of antibodies [206], carbohydrates [207], and protein ligands [208] allows the functionalization of AuNPs to develop adequate methodologies and improve the treatment and diagnosis, preferentially with the same NPs. Surface modification not only affects the quality of NPs (such as stability and monodispersity) but also determines the sensitivity and specificity of the AuNPs, through the exhibition of functional groups or charges that can be used for conjugation [209]. AuNPs coated with PEG and pH Low Insertion Peptide (pHLIP) were synthetized. pHLIP allowed tumor-targeting and improved the stability of nanoparticles in solution, also stimulating a pH-dependent cellular uptake. AuNPs were homogeneously distributed within a tumor mass without NPs in the muscle tissue near the tumor. Besides, the irradiation of AuNPs by a laser led to a time- and concentration-dependent increase of temperature [210]. Functionalized AuNPs, with DNA, have been developed. They increased the synthesis of proteins with the potential of treatment of diseases associated with unregulated proteins in the cells [211,212]. Avila et al. studied [213] AuNPs functionalized with organic molecules (4-aminothiphenol and cysteamine), and their toxicity was assessed. Furthermore, the AuNPs revealed low polydispersity and an excellent colloidal stability, and stability in cell culture medium was also confirmed. The fluorescence images denoted the cellular uptake of AuNPs without visible damages. However, these AuNPs need to be submitted to more experiments to ensure their safety for biomedical applications. The size and charge of NPs were assessed by various research groups in terms of cellular uptake. However, an opposite behavior was observed. As expected, higher uptake happened not only with positive-charged particles but also with increasing of particle size. In fact, controversially, the zwitterionic and anionic particle uptake decreased with increasing AuNPs size [214]. This may be explained by the uptake pathways; passive diffusion is governed by zwitterionic NPs, while multiple endocytic pathways are directed by cationic and anionic NPs. In the literature, many studies established the influence of particle size in cellular uptake and also the growth of cells. Ko et al. demonstrated [215] that the 30 and 50nm AuNPs were preferentially up taken into the cells; all sizes of AuNPs promoted the osteogenic differentiation, but the 30 and 50nm AuNPs proved to have the highest differentiation rates. However, in the cytotoxicity profile, size was not a problem. The different charges of functionalized AuNPs demonstrated no cytotoxicity and were well tolerated. As expected, positively charged AuNPs display a superior cellular uptake, due to the higher affinity to the negative charges of membrane cells by electrostatic attraction. COOH-AuNPs, negatively charged NPs, reduced the alkaline phosphatase activity and calcium deposition [216]. Liu et al. studied [217] the influence of particle size and charge at the same time in two different cells, in nonphagocytic HepG2 cells and phagocytic RAW 264.7 cells. The results varied not only with charge and size but also with cell type. Positive-AuNPs possessed a higher cell internalization ability than negative-AuNPs in nonphagocytic HepG2 cells, as confirmed by other studies. Conversely, the uptake of negative-AuNPs was similar to that of positive-AuNPs in phagocytic RAW 264.7 cells. The study allowed to conclude that interaction between AuNPs and cells is not only affected by size or the surface factors but also depends on cell types. Another approach used AuNPs-based vaccines and assessed the effect of size on induction of CD8+ T-cell responses. Kang et al. synthesized AuNPs, and then conjugated them with recombinant albumin (OVA); consequently, the size of the OVA-AuNPs increased. Of note, 22 and 33nm OVA-AuNPs displayed a higher delivery efficiency to draining lymph nodes than did smaller size particles; additionally, 22 and 33nm OVA-AuNPs showed higher induction of T-cell responses than smaller OVA-AuNPs. Considering all results, the particle size is a critical parameter in designing and developing AuNPs-based vaccines [218].

There is incomplete information regarding the impact of pregnancy on AuNPs biodistribution and toxicity. Yang et al. investigated [219] the biodistribution and toxic effects of different-sized AuNPs during pregnancy and at different gestational phases. Despite the results have revealed size-dependent biodistribution profiles, biodistribution did not change between nonpregnant and pregnant mice. The smallest size showed primarily excretion through urine, whereas 30nm AuNPs had a more prolonged blood circulation time. Again, size did not influence the toxic effects in pregnant mice. Furthermore, 30nm AuNPs administered to pregnant mice can induce mild emphysema-like changes in the lungs. Here, extra studies are required to understand the mechanisms subjacent to potential toxicity in lungs. Additionally, in vitro studies were performed in human placental, to evaluate the impact of particle size and surface modification of AuNPs.
