The cellular uptake and the penetration were higher for smaller AuNPs (3–4nm) or sodium carboxylate-modified AuNPs than for bigger AuNPs (13–14nm) or PEGylated. Lastly, as observed with other nanocarriers, PEGylation of AuNP-drug carriers might be used as an approach to keep drugs in the maternal circulation to specifically treat pregnant women [220]. Some parameters, such as direct electron transfer rate or the biocatalytic activity of the adsorbed enzyme, are not influenced by the size [221].

A large number of researches are focused on in vitro and in vivo toxicity of AuNPs; however, data are controversial. Thus, the AuNPs toxicity is still incomplete and inconsistent, due to the variety in experimental environments and also the diverse AuNPs composition [222]. Despite dissimilarities in the size of the AuNPs used, and in the different cell types, the results present in the literature did not enable one to obtain meaningful conclusions concerning particle size and toxicity. There is an assortment of shape of AuNPs, and their complexity increases due to their needs in bioapplications: nanorods, nanoprisms, nanoshells, nanocages, hollow nanostructures, and nanostars (Fig. 4.11 ). The shapes of AuNPs influence their toxic effect [229]. A recent study indicated that the spherical and star-shaped AuNPs revealed the fastest internalization, followed by the cubic, then rod- and disk-like AuNPs. Wang et al. described a comparison between different shapes of AuNPs and assessed their in vitro and in vivo performance. Although all these AuNPs could absorb and convert NIR light into heat, AuNP nanohexapods with a similar structure to nanostars, presented the maximum cellular uptake and the smallest cytotoxicity in vitro; in vivo pharmacokinetic studies displayed that the functionalized AuNPs nanohexapods displayed a higher blood circulation and tumor accumulation; additionally, photothermal treatment had a similar action with all AuNPs [230]. Another study was performed to assess the influence of various shapes of AuNPs in terms of toxicity, biodistribution, and cellular uptake in adult zebrafish. The results showed a nontoxicity of the AuNPs; rod-AuNPs showed rapid uptake and clearance; conversely, star-AuNPs showed slow and long uptake compared to the other two shapes [231].

The goal of NPs is to reach the diseased cells after administration. The in vivo studies allow to predict if the nanocarriers accomplish the task. The coating and targeting approaches are crucial for increasing the drug efficacy and decreasing the side effects, once it is avoided the nonspecific uptake and degradation in macrophages. Therefore, a new approach has been explored and described in the literature as hybrid-NPs that combine the safety of organic NPs with imaging properties of inorganic NPs, thus allowing synergistic properties in a unique NP (Table 4.1). A novel strategy of encapsulating AuNPs, with different charges, within multi-lamellar LPs, and electrostatic interactions between negative charged lipids and positively charged and vice versa was used as driving force to facilitate the encapsulation of AuNPs within LPs (Fig. 4.12 ). This combination showed to be promising and should be manipulated and suitably controlled. Also, it will be important to assess their cytotoxicity, biodistribution, and pharmacokinetics behavior [232].

Beyond the encapsulation capability, AuNPs are also useful as a biosensor, due to their optical and electronic properties, their biocompatibility, and their easy production and surface modification. AuNPs have unique specificities that allow the recognition of the corresponding analytes in some diseases. These analytes have biological origin, such as DNAs, proteins, antibodies, and antigens. The biggest challenge in biosensor elaboration is the efficient signal transduction. The interaction of the analyte with the biological element is transducted into electrochemical, electrochemiluminescent, magnetic, or optical signals. The use of AuNPs as biosensors allows low cost, and rapid, direct, and visual detection methods [233–237].

AuNPs’ performance is promising because they have a high quantity of protons and neutrons in their nucleus. Thus, when AuNPs are exposed to electromagnetic radiation, the excitation of electrons produces strong surface fields, which leads to important release of heat upon relaxation. The heat generated is enough to destroy tissues [238]. As previous described, these preclinical data are favorable and exciting for the translation of AuNPs (Table 4.2). CYT-6091 is an AuNP, which incorporates a recombinant human tumor necrosis factor alpha (rhTNF) and thiolyated PEG, and has a particle size of 27nm. Previous clinical trials of TNF-alpha display dose-limiting toxicities of hypotension and nausea, which limited more extensive clinical use. CYT-6091 not only led to a decrease in toxicity when compared with rhTNF but also showed a high targeting to tumors [239]. NU-0129 consists of nucleic acids organized on the surface of small spherical AuNPs. When the nanosystem is inside the tumor, the nucleic acid is able to target a gene called Bcl2L12, present in GBM, and helps stop cancer cells from growing.

QDNPs are semiconductor nanocrystals, with dimensions between about 1 and 60nm, and can be recognized as the smallest platform for NP-based drug delivery engineering. Depending on their size, each QDNPs will have a broad absorption spectra. Their interesting optimal properties, photostability, make them extraordinarily applicable for in vitro and in vivo biomedical imaging, as in vitro diagnostics, cellular and in vivo imaging, drug delivery and theranostics [240,241]. Presently, they are gradually emerging in biomedical imaging areas and enriching the performance of some devices, with accessible methods and low cost. Also, QDNPs are easily incorporated within NDS with minimal effect on characteristics of both, and offer excellent optical properties for real-time monitoring of NDS, studying the behavior, cellular uptake, drug release, and biodistribution at both cellular and systemic levels [35,242]. QDNPs have a versatile surface, due to the anchor points presented by metal atoms on the surface for attachment of a diversity of surface ligands [243].

QDNPs in biomedical sensing have shown higher selectivity, sensitivity, and efficiency over conventional methods. Besides, they afford detections of multiple biomarkers. The combination of aptamers and QDNPs as a biosensor for tumor cell recognition and detection was developed. The high sensibility will be beneficial for premature diagnosis of cancer [251]. Yuan et al. developed an ultrasensitive fluorescence biosensor for detection of miRNA based on targeting-triggered DNA-QDs and DNAzyme-modulated double quenching of QDs. This approach displayed a high amplification signal skill, specificity, and efficiency to quenching QDs, due to the DNAzyme-modulated double quenching mechanism [252]. Combination strategies based on magnetic particles and QDs for improved biosensing have been described. Also, it has been reported the both strategies have higher performance, contributing to easier and more sensitive bioanalyses.

The assessment of the potential toxicity of QDs, their biodistribution, and pharmacokinetic parameters are crucial for the clinical application. To evaluate the effect of surface chemistry and charge on the in vivo biodistribution and toxicity, CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs and coated with PEG were synthetized, with positive and negative charge. The biodistribution depends on the surface properties. Positive-QDNPs had a preferential deposition in the lung, and severe acute toxicity due to pulmonary embolism; while the negative and PEGylated QDs had high accumulation in the liver. PEGylated QDNPs showed the thinnest chronic injuries in the long-term toxicity, when compared to the others [253]. The difficulty in approval of QDs and their clinical use is because they incorporate the toxic heavy metal cadmium, and their individual ions, such as Cd2+, Se2+, and Te2+, within the core of the QDs. To overcome this obstacle, Yaghini et al. developed bio-QDs. These QDs use indium as semiconductor nanocrystals. The bio-nanosystem maintained the surface modification ability and also its photostability. The bio-nanosystem suffered a high retention in to regional lymph nodes. This methodology has low intrinsic toxicity and, consequently, is promising to biomedical applications [254]. Another modification on the heavy metal that composed QDs was developed and their in vivo performance was evaluated. Ag2S QDs were considered less toxic than the reported PbSe, PbS, and CdHgTe QDs. Ag2S QDs functionalized with PEG showed accumulation in the tumor area; the higher accumulation happened in the liver and can be attributed to the high early uptake of PEGylated-Ag2S QDs. by the RES. Still, the clearance mechanism and genotoxicity of PEGylated-Ag2S were also imperative and demand further investigation [255]. QDNPs with diverse surface coatings displayed considerably different biodistribution and in vivo toxicity. This is a huge problem owing to the complexity of the system. Depending on the nature of the QDNPs, they will be taken up through different mechanisms, and the way as the QDNPs will be taken rules the decomposition or degradability of the QDs. Consequently, there are different toxicities [256]. QDNPs are also applied in personalized therapy. Manshian et al. synthetized and evaluated the performance of CdTe QDs in a personalized NP-mediated therapy [41]. They concluded that the administered dose was related to the size of the tumor, consequently, dosages administered are well tailored. The personalized approach presented avoids the different results between animals in the same group and provides more reproducible result. Additionally, the wide biological variability typically associated with tumor studies is overcome [257].

Concerns about toxicological and pharmacological issues mostly from heavy metal instability are major limitations of the advancement in the diagnosis and therapy of cancer and other diseases and are also barriers for human application. Despite efforts to decrease toxicity and improve detection properties, based on surface modifications, control on their components, using a “friendly” heavy metal, modulation on size and stability, it seems not to be enough to allow QDNPs approval by regulatory authorities. The increasing complexity of QDNPs deemed from the diverse functionalization strategies produces different fluorescence quantum. Therefore, the establishment of quality criteria is a problem in terms of reproducibility and reliability. A significant amount of biodistribution studies revealed nonspecific accumulation by the RES, including the liver, spleen, lymphatic system, and less in the kidney. The biosafety for in vivo applications, toxicological and pharmacokinetic explorations including degradation, excretion, endurance, and immune response of QDNPs should be methodically assessed. The inefficient delivery, potential toxicity, and lack of quantification are also major barriers to clinical translation of QDNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NDS in different therapeutic areas
ce:section.ce:para: The growing use of nanotechnology in every area of the human healthcare is noteworthy. This clearly indicates that nanotechnology will continue to be a substantial component of research over the decades to come, and probably mostly directed at cancer therapy. Three main areas will be addressed and some challenges and strategies discussed to understand what are the hurdles associated to the translation of NDS.

Despite the huge development and progress in cancer therapy, cancer continues to be the highest cause of death worldwide. Cancer is a heterogeneous disease, with multiple cell phenotypes in a unique tumor, which results in problematic treatment. However, the NPs as a delivery system allows a unique approach for cancer treatment, helping to overcome several challenges associated with conventional chemotherapeutics, including effectiveness, short drug half-life, drug resistance, nonspecific biodistribution, increased toxicity, NPs internalization, intracellular drug concentration, co-delivery of multiple drugs or contrast agents by manifold signaling pathways and physiological barriers. Also, a favorable approach to improve the performance of NPs is to target signaling pathways exploiting a combination of drugs while controlling drug release, in terms of distribution, timing, and dose. Consequently, the targeting approach enables the modification of drug pharmacokinetics and biodistribution, advanced efficacy and reduces the side effects. The fast advance in nanotechnology has allowed a development of complex NPs which can integrate both imaging and therapeutic functionalities. It is possible to prescribe a theranostic treatment with visualization of the NPs within the body, understand the exact site of accumulation, and also confirm the release of the drug, monitoring the treatment efficiency of the NPs.

Metastasis is the main cause of death in cancer patients, and there are currently research addressing the use of NPs for treatment of metastatic cancer. However, preclinical efficacy is compromised due to the limitations of artificial models. While primary tumors have a high amount of blood neovasculature, the metastatic ones have diminished blood flow or nonfunctional blood vessels. Consequently, the efficacy of therapy with NPs is affected, because it depends on perfusion for transport to the targeting site. Therefore, a different strategy needs to be adopted. Considering that NPs have shown tumor-specific targeting in different organs, this strategy can be used to accomplish effectiveness in treating metastatic lesions. The latest functionalized approaches in NPs have shown extraordinary results in animal models, [312,313] which is very promising for the eventual clinical application of theranostics.

In the section 4.2, several NPs under investigation are described, and also the NPs already implemented on the clinic (Table 4.2). Most research data, clinical trials, and clinical drug approval pertain to cancer treatment or diagnosis, showing the strong potential within this area and the interest it arises. Comprising organic and inorganic NPs, there are currently 183 clinical trials, with 43 recruiting, 27 active, 4 suspended, 10 withdrawn, 17 terminated, 14 with unknown status, 58 completed, and only 14 have results and 10 not yet recruiting [314]. Although there are numerous NPs technologies under development, most do not possess a preclinical proof of concept yet, due to absent correlation between the in vitro and the in vivo results (Fig. 4.14 ). The lack of knowledge on the interactions between NPs and cells, and the protein corona formation are obstacles for the advancement of clinical trials, because they make the in vivo NPs performance unpredictable from the in vitro stages. Biodistribution, toxicity, and pharmacokinetic studies must be considered in all steps of the development of the NPs formulation and limit the translation of oncological nanomedicines into clinical practice. Personalized oncology may be a solution in the near future, as will be discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Disorders of the central nervous system
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of the BBB creates a formidable challenge for drug delivery to the CNS. The low efficiency of the treatment options of CNS disorders has led to the development of novel nanotheranostic strategies. Thus, nanosystems are used in the CNS with regeneration and neuroprotection as the main goals. The research efforts in nanotechnology have resulted in the production of highly advanced nanomedicines, configured for targeted delivery across the BBB [315]. The surface of NPs can be modified and groups can be attached, so engineering a targeted NPs to the CNS via specific BBB mechanisms is possible [316,317]. NPs have a large potential for encapsulation, which is important for applications as the delivery of cytotoxic agents, neurotrophic peptides, enzymes, gene vectors, and other molecules that pose especial difficulties to the brain.

AD is a neurodegenerative condition of the aged and represents the most predominant form of dementia, being associated to a dysfunctional interaction of the protein beta amyloid. It provided a fertile ground for the use of NPs to improve and regulate multiple pathological dysfunctions and overcome barriers in drug delivery. There are numerous NPs under investigation, including PNPs with the dual function of delivering gene and peptides [318–322], or particles that combine the optical properties of AuNPs with the high affinity of biological molecules resulting in a simple visual method to determine the relative amounts of components on the neuronal cell surface [323]. Also noteworthy are the applications of IONPs conjugated with curcumin, possessing antiamyloid and antioxidant activity, binding to the amyloid plaques and enabling visualizing the plates [324], SLN designed to transport BACE1 siRNA able to overcome the barriers [325], or LPs functionalized to promote high-affinity beta amyloid binding [30]. The translation of AD NPs is more difficult and several questions need to be answered.

Huntington’s disease (HD) is a neurodegenerative disease-causing pathogenesis resulting in mitochondrial dysfunction and bioenergetics failure. Currently, some NPs are developed to treat HD, including SLN as an approach for an effective management in HD [326,327]. Also, PNPs can inhibit amyloid/polyglutamine aggregation, reduce aggregation-derived cytotoxicity, and prevent polyglutamine aggregation [328]. IONPs reduce brain damage and enable the use of NPs in cell tracking [329], and AuNPs were used as a biosensor platform for colorimetric detection of active DNA methyltransferase/glycosylase [330]. A different approach was introduced through an overview of stem cell therapy for the HD, using NPs for cell programming in vitro combined with siRNA/pre-miRNA [331].

For a better understanding of passive permeation across the BBB, it is important to identify the role played by drug receptors and drug sites of action. All knowledge associated with advances in medicinal chemistry makes it possible to engineer potential NPs with greatly enhanced activity and selectivity. However, most CNS disorders are chronic, they may require long and continuous treatment, resulting in the accumulation of materials associated to NPs. Additionally, the effectiveness of NDS is often based on inappropriate animal models. Problems, such as accumulation, toxicity, metabolism, and clearance need to be understood and studied. Therefore, a systematic and comprehensive evaluation of NPs neurotoxic effects is specifically important for decreasing or preventing CNS damage.

Only eight clinical trials are currently proceeding, with three recruiting, one terminated, one completed, one withdrawn, and two which have been approved but are not yet recruiting. The majority of clinical trials focus on the characterization of brain tumors using contrast agents, with one addressing autoimmune diseases [332]. There is still a long way to go before the clinical stage to assess potential efficacy and toxicity problems [333].

The IS is responsible for protecting the host from environmental agents, such as microbes or chemicals, and preserving the integrity of the body. The interactions between NPs and components of the IS have become an active area of research in nanotechnology [334]. The research has shown that engineered surfaces can change the properties of the NPs, enabling to avoid or specifically target the IS [335], including vaccine delivery, and improving the quality of antiinflammatory, anticancer, and antiviral therapies [143,336,337]. Besides, pathogens become resistant to drugs by different strategies, including DNA alterations, modifications in membrane permeability, development of multidrug efflux pumps, and activation of enzymes responsible for drug degradation or increase of their intracellular lifecycle [338]. Therefore, targeted NPs protect the drug from resistance mechanisms, so as to reach therapeutic intracellular levels [339,340]. The particle size and the shape of NPs are crucial properties in the application of NPs in IS disorders. The interaction of IS-NPs is governed by size and shape and, consequently, the immune response will be also affected [341,342].

AuNPs are described as promising NPs for cancer immunotherapy, with demonstrated efficacy in immune delivery and innate cell activation [343,344]. The AuNPs are used as adjuvant in drug delivery and to boost the efficacy of a free antigen; also, AuNPs demonstrated a higher inflammatory response in vitro than PEGylated NPs. The application of vaccines in cancer treatment is promising, because they boost tumor-specific CD8+T cells, crucially dependent on suitable delivery of the vaccine to dendritic cells. PNPs were developed to induce potent CD8+ T cell responses by CD40-targeted dendritic cells [345]. SLNs are used to encapsulate lipophilic drugs, as curcumin, and used for sepsis treatment. The NPs system decreases the expression levels of TLR4, TLR2, and TNF-α in lymph node tissues, and suppresses of NF-κB activation and IκBα degradation levels [346]. Another application of LNs consisted of delivering siRNAs to murine, being used a surface-functionalized with the anti-CD4 monoclonal antibody [347]. The manipulation of T-cell functionality and the maintenance of RNAi in the cells by LN is a promising strategy for leukocyte-associated diseases.

Again, and although some research is being conducted, inappropriate animal models are often used, and there is no correlation between the in vivo animal studies and the in vivo in humans. Also, the problems of NPs toxicity and clearance need to be understood. There are only 12 clinical trials described, with 3 active, 1 recruiting, 1 terminated, 2 completed, 1 suspended, 2 enrolling by invitation, and 2 not yet recruiting [348]. Most of the clinical trials are dedicated to cancer treatment, while diabetes, multiple sclerosis, and autoimmune encephalitis are also under investigation, using NPs as Abraxane, Ferumoxytol, C19 A3 AuNPs, and IONPs.

One question can now be posed: what is needed to develop NPs that go into clinic faster? It is well established that translation of basic scientific ideas to practice and clinic application is a long, expensive, and challenging path. Focusing on engineering of NPs, what targeting approaches may be used, what is the behavior in the human body, and how the NPs will be scaled up, are some critical points that must be addressed to minimize development time. Fig. 4.15 represents the most important barriers to NPs commercialization. In addition, FDA recommends meticulous characterization of NPs with respect to surface chemistry, crystallinity, and aggregation because, as mentioned, the in vivo behavior of NPs is different from the behavior in the laboratory, and it is needed to carefully assess biodistribution, metabolism, clearance or biopersistence. Biopersistence is the ability of the NPs to remain in the body and resist chemical degradation. This biological process is dominated by the complex and dynamic interactions with components of the biological media. Despite several investigations on NPs characterization and numerous in vitro and in vivo studies, many questions remain without answers, such as the influence of biopersistence of NPs. There is ambiguity in the criteria applied to characterize the NPs as biopersistent. Recognition and quantification of NPs and also their transformation remains a challenge for human toxicology. Apart from composition, NPs biopersistence or biodegradability is influenced by the complex and dynamic interactions with components of the biological media, by their structure and the nature of the surface coating. The knowledge about the interaction is a crucial step towards determining the biological interactions NPs may have in vivo. In addition to the lack of knowledge, an important factor is the financial requirements for covering all aspects in these studies. Also, the delay for clinical development can be associated with issues of NPs characterization, large-scale preparation, and reproducibility, and obtaining data regarding the safety of the NPs (Fig. 4.15). The regulatory approval of NPs is challenging, because of higher complexity of the nanocarrier, making the approval process even more complicated for multifunctional NPs involving therapeutic, diagnostic/imaging, and targeting agents. Despite progress in nanomedicine, only a small number of NPs have been evaluated in clinical trials, and fewer have been extended to clinical practice (Table 4.2). Several factors have impact on this incomplete response. These include genotype, and nongenetic and environmental factors, including sex, age, diet, and lifestyle. It has long been accepted that patients respond differently to the same drug in terms of efficacy and safety. To bridge this translational gap resorting to personalized medicine seems to be a substantial innovation, with the goal of improving the targeting approach [349]. Personalized medicine means the prescription of therapeutics directed at each individual. Pharmacogenetics, pharmacogenomics, and pharmacoproteomic information are crucial for defining this type of therapy; they also enable the redefinition of molecular diagnostic and the discovery of biomarkers. The application of personalized medicine is promising for cancer, because of its heterogeneity. Personalized medicine can also enable a better understanding of the disease at a molecular level. The use of NPs associated to personalized medicine is hopeful to optimize treatment protocols tailored to each patient, stimulate the body to do a raised response [350]. Besides, it makes possible to decrease the side effects, with less tissue and organ destruction, and at the same time increase the localization of pharmaceutical drugs in the diseased tissue. Although important challenges exist, namely regulatory approval and scientific challenges associated with manufacturing NPs, deployment of personalized nanomedicines will be a promise for treatment of complex diseases. Thus, it is predictable that personalized medicine, pharmacogenomics, and pharmacogenetics associated with NDS improve their therapeutic efficacy and minimize the challenges related to the implementation of NPs into commercially successful clinical product.

The in silico studies, with computational models, to predict the pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and toxicity behavior of NPs are essential in multiple stages of NPs development [351–355]. It is possible to understand how NPs physical properties influence pharmacokinetics and biodistribution in different organisms. The strategy not only reduces the number of animals needed in preclinical stage but also simplifies the development process and estimate the pharmacokinetic behavior in humans, helping principally the populations that are not included in clinical trials, as children and pregnant women. Although the potential of this tool is high, there are barriers associated with its use, including the failure in mechanistic knowledge and the complexity of the NPs formulation. The application on pharmacokinetics modulation will benefit the development of NPs, their market approval and regulatory entities and, consequently, translational approaches can lead to quicker and safer development of NPs for clinical applications.

Financial challenges increase mainly for university researchers and members of small/medium size enterprises. Also, scaling up within good manufacturing practices requires large production cycles and, on top of this, it is necessary human and financial resources. Industrial support is important for the translation of NPs. Patent protection for NPs under investigation is another critical point. This defines stockholders’ interest and researchers’ opportunity. Another question is the lack of a clear and broadly recognized definition of nanomedicine from a regulatory perspective. However, it is notorious that nanomedicines are frequently not within the 1–100nm size range, principally when the investigation uses organic NPs with surface modifications. As mentioned throughout the text, the size is a crucial NPs property, because it defines the NPs’ behavior in terms of therapeutic efficiency, biodistribution and toxicity, and pathways mechanism. For that reason, the dialogue between academia, industry, and regulatory agencies is also important in the complete process, as those knowledge gaps are developed by academic research expertise or sometimes by industry, thus overcoming technical issues that limit the clinical translation of nanomedicines, as consistent and validated in vitro assays for testing safety and efficacy; in vitro/ex vivo models applicable to several routes of administration; better understanding of the pharmacokinetic behavior in vivo, and their connection to NPs characteristics; development of computational approaches to forecast the biological and toxicological effects in the human body; and understanding of the factors that define interactions between NPs cells.

It is becoming gradually clearer by the number of publications, recent development, and clinical trials, that nanotechnology will play a fundamental role in the progress of the next generation of multifunctional hybrid NPs. However, the successful clinical translation of therapeutic NPs involves optimization of many different parameters, such as the composition of the nanocarrier system, surface charge, particle size, EE of drug or contrast agent, density of possible ligands for targeting, stability, interaction with the cells, specificity for targeting cells, and also the release of drug/contrast agents. Thus, there is a wealth of potential variables for optimization which is impossible to address using a simple throughput approach. Moreover, one of the major failures in the translation of NPs for medical applications concerns safety issues. However, the clinical use demands meticulous, prolonged evaluation of the toxicity of NPs, and their potential immunogenicity. Concerning the engineering of NPs with selective physicochemical properties for clinical applications, it is crucial to set formulation homogeneity in preclinical trials. However, considering a complex medium, the heterogeneous human population, and all of the relevant physicochemical properties, it is difficult to obtain reproducibility. Still, NPs can be engineered to interact with specific biological components, enabling them to benefit from the perceptions provided by personalized medicine therapy. With an increasing understanding at the molecular level of health and disease, the potential of NPs to address the biological complexities of diseases will also increase. This also applies to the opportunities to develop patient- and disease-specific theranostic modalities.

The engineering, development, and commercialization of NPs clinically used need collaboration between three major groups: research investigators, investors/companies, and regulatory authorities. These close collaboration and transfer of the experience and knowledge to the development of nanomedicines, consequentially essential for an effective translation. In the coming years, the global perspective for the use of NDS is clearly optimistic, in multifunctional applications, and in combination with personalized medicine strategies.
